key_insight: Experimental inorganic materials discovery exhibits strong structural memory, and modern computed proposals often fall outside those historical experimental basins.
engineering_relevance: A historical synthesizability prior can help materials engineers triage millions of computed candidates toward compounds more likely to be experimentally realizable.
method: Embedded 167,500 ICSD structures into a continuous structural-similarity space.
Partitioned the space into graph communities and replayed discovery over time.
Projected computed datasets into frozen historical maps to compare their structural proximity to experimental basins.
evidence_signal: New formulas overwhelmingly entered pre-existing communities (82.9%), new-community formation dropped from 40.2% in the 1930s to 2.6% in the 2010s, and held-out ICSD ranked above MatterGen, GNoME/MP-theoretical, JARVIS, and Alexandria.
watch_item: The ordering depends on the chosen structural embedding and historical reference frame, so synthesizability scores should be treated as a triage prior rather than a direct measure of experimental success.
paper_title: Computed materials proposals depart from the structural memory of experimental discovery
